skill.name: clickup-automation
skill.description: Automate ClickUp tasks, hierarchy lookup, comments, team queries, and filtered task searches through the Rube MCP ClickUp toolkit.
skill.summary: Use Rube MCP tools to manage ClickUp workspaces, lists, tasks, comments, and team data.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium issues, but the shell execution and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and ordinary text. The real risk is medium because the skill requires Rube MCP network access and can modify or delete real ClickUp data through an authenticated connection.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Third-Party ClickUp Automation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to use Rube MCP and an active ClickUp connection to create, update, comment on, and query ClickUp records. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can modify real workspace data through an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network dependency and authenticated ClickUp operations are explicit in the instructions. The behavior is expected for the skill, but it creates real data-change risk.

medium_findings.title: Destructive ClickUp Task Deletion Capability
medium_findings.description: The guide lists CLICKUP_DELETE_TASK as an optional operation and describes task deletion in the quick reference. Accidental or prompt-driven deletion could permanently remove ClickUp tasks if the connected account is authorized.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deletion tool is directly named in task management and quick reference sections. The risk is clear, although it is a legitimate ClickUp management feature.

low_findings.title: Inline MCP Tool Names Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported many Ruby or shell backtick executions, but the referenced lines are Markdown inline code spans naming Rube MCP and ClickUp tools. No shell command, script, or executable code path is present in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation and the flagged backticks wrap tool names and parameter names. I found no Ruby, shell, or command execution syntax.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Is a Text Match False Positive
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm locations correspond to normal prose and parameter text such as description fields, not cryptographic operations. No hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or credential handling code appears in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive ClickUp text, not code that invokes DES, MD5, SHA1, or another weak primitive. The static pattern appears to match substrings in ordinary words.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Pattern Is ClickUp Data Lookup
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance locations describe ClickUp task tracking and workspace lookup workflows. They query ClickUp resources through authorized tools and do not inspect the host system, local network, processes, files, or environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are project-management instructions inside ClickUp. I found no host reconnaissance commands or local system enumeration behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is a Documented MCP Endpoint
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to the Rube MCP server setup endpoint used by the skill. This is a third-party network dependency, but there is no evidence of hidden exfiltration or unauthorized callback behavior in the skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external URL is clearly present and central to setup. Its purpose is documented, so the risk is dependency and data-routing exposure rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: ClickUp work often requires repeated navigation through workspaces, spaces, folders, lists, and task records. This skill provides structured Rube MCP workflows for reliable ClickUp updates.
actual_capabilities: Find ClickUp workspaces, spaces, folders, folderless lists, and lists before acting.
Create tasks and subtasks with statuses, assignees, dates, priorities, tags, and estimates.
Update task status, priority, assignees, dates, and descriptions through ClickUp tools.
Add, list, and update task comments with required assignment and notification fields.
Query workspace seats, user groups, users, roles, and filtered task lists.
limitations: Requires Rube MCP and an active ClickUp OAuth connection.
Depends on current Rube tool schemas, so tools must be searched before use.
Can affect real ClickUp data, including task deletion, when authorized.
Enterprise-only ClickUp endpoints may fail on lower account plans.

use_cases.title: Create Project Tasks
use_cases.description: Create tasks and subtasks in the correct ClickUp list after resolving workspace and list IDs.

use_cases.title: Review Team Capacity
use_cases.description: Check workspace seats, user groups, and role information before planning assignments.

use_cases.title: Audit Work In Progress
use_cases.description: Filter tasks by status, assignee, due date, tag, or page to review current delivery work.

prompt_templates.title: Find a ClickUp List
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the ClickUp list named <list name> in workspace <workspace name>. Show the workspace, space, folder, and list IDs before taking action.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a ClickUp task in <list name> called <task name>. Use status <status>, assignee <user>, due date <date>, and notify watchers only if I confirm.

prompt_templates.title: Update Comments
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a comment to task <task id> that assigns <user> and summarizes <message>. First confirm the task exists and ask before notifying all watchers.

prompt_templates.title: Bulk Planning Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review open tasks in <list name> due between <start date> and <end date>. Group them by assignee and flag missing priority or stale status.

output_examples.input: Find the onboarding list and create tasks for the first week of a new hire plan.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the workspace path, confirms the target list, then proposes named tasks with assignees, due dates, and statuses before creation.

output_examples.input: Show overdue high priority tasks for the mobile team.
output_examples.output: The assistant resolves the relevant list, applies due date and priority filters, and returns a concise task review grouped by assignee.

output_examples.input: Add a review comment to task 86abc and assign it to Priya.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks that the task exists, prepares the comment text, confirms notification settings, and then posts the assigned comment.

best_practices: Search Rube tools first so parameter schemas match the current ClickUp connector.
Resolve names to IDs before creating, updating, commenting on, or deleting ClickUp records.
Ask for confirmation before bulk changes, watcher notifications, or destructive operations.
anti_patterns: Do not guess workspace, space, folder, list, task, or user IDs from names alone.
Do not run retries for task creation without checking whether a task was already created.
Do not delete tasks or notify all watchers without explicit user approval.

faq.question: Does this skill require a ClickUp connection?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires Rube MCP and an active ClickUp OAuth connection through the ClickUp toolkit.

faq.question: Can it create and update real ClickUp tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. When authorized, it can create tasks, update task fields, add comments, and perform other ClickUp actions.

faq.question: Can it delete tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. The guide includes a ClickUp delete task operation, so deletion should require explicit confirmation.

faq.question: Why must tools be searched first?
faq.answer: Rube tool schemas can change. Searching first helps the assistant use current parameter names and requirements.

faq.question: Does it manage ClickUp permissions?
faq.answer: It can query seats, user groups, users, and custom roles when the connected account and plan allow those endpoints.

faq.question: Is this a standalone ClickUp client?
faq.answer: No. It is an instruction guide for using Rube MCP tools from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code.
